Preliminary-Findings-Material-Identity-P.title: Project Report - Materiality and Identity
Preliminary-Findings-Material-Identity-P.content:
Introduction
Formulating the question and identifying stakeholders
Some thoughts about method
The interview
Preliminary findings and observations
References



home
other writing


Introduction
The research question that forms the basis of this report is exploring how individual subjects who come from various geographic, political, religious, class, caste and other social positions in the South Asian region, and are likely to represent a unique overlap of these, are able to navigate the social space of a university in the UK. The common linchpin that is the basis to understand these subjects is the way they eat food. The project uses semi-structured interviews and sensory emplacement in order to achieve this, where the interview is perceived as a multisensory event (Pink, 2015) and is conducted while participants cook their food. The interviews are subjected to thematic analysis with inductive coding and highlighting of themes and sub-themes within them. The transcriptions also contain multisensorial detail of sounds and activity that occurred during the interview outside formal lines of questioning.
This report is based on three interviews conducted as part of a postgraduate course on Qualitative Social Research Methods at the London School of Economics, and outlines the thought process that went into the research design for this project, and while three interviews is not sufficient evidence to hypothesise anything of grander value, it presented me with a complex and rich amount of qualitative data that allowed me to consider preliminary findings and record them in this document. The relevance of this research is to notice how changing social contexts amid complex personal cultural histories allow for disjunctive experiences of student life in London. Centering the interview around food also potentially indicate research on material culture and consumption practices in this disjunctive context.
Formulating the question and identifying stakeholders
My approach to this research question came from a personal place. Food is a part of everyday life. Human (and non-human) lives revolve around food, and this is something I have always found intriguing. My own personal relationship with food involved experiencing food in a highly religious Hindu environment where vegetarian food was constructed in terms of purity. I then went on to unlearn those purist notions. And when I started working in the environment and animal rights sector, I turned vegan. In all of this, the South Asian diaspora is a cultural amalgam of very complex identities and hierarchies, very different from what I experienced in my interactions with people in the UK.
Right off the bat, I realised that the broader my research was, the less likely was I to arrive at something productive. In engaging with literature in urban theory (Harvey, 2012) and its intersection with cultural studies (Holston & Appadurai, 1996), I realised the city was a site where cultural contexts coalesce in a generally homogenised way. In this paradigm, through personal engagements in student activism, I realised that there are cultural differences that could arise even in terms of the social position of a person. While metrics like age, sex and gender played a role, I developed a hunch that a student and a working professional were likely to approach their lives very differently when it came to food, and were also therefore likely to care about things differently. So slowly, my research narrowed down to students (typically from a similar cultural place – like the South Asian region) living in a city on their own.
I initially wanted my project to have something to do with veganism and my personal disillusionment with the cognitive dissonance (Rothgerber, 2020) everyone around me seemed to exhibit, where personal engagement with animal rights might position someone as caring for all the right things (they might even have pets of their own) and seem a genuine and empathetic person, but they at the same time would disregard their own relationship with their own food – most predominantly meat, and when a conversation about factory farming would unsettle their eating practices, which seemed almost psychologically emplaced within their daily life, the inconvenience they would experience clouded their ability to hold a conversation about it.
But I realised that conducting interviews with a sample size of people specifically about veganism was likely to run into two problems. Firstly, I was at a risk of conflating my interviewee’s experiences with my own veganism, and that would create a kind of bias likely to defeat the purpose of collecting qualitative data from a subject. Secondly, it would be impractical to center an interview on veganism because if I were to interview a vegan person, I would likely find them agreeing with me, while a non-vegan would likely be made to feel uncomfortable in such a setting. I could not influence an interview in any particular way, I needed to have a research question that was open-ended enough to give my participants space to express their own lived experience.
Patton (2002), in discussing kinds of qualitative interviewing, talks of a “truly open-ended question” being necessary for valuable qualitative research. My question was not open-ended at all. It presumed some things to be right and worked within that. Evolving through this, I decided to abandon my own veganism for the moment and work with the idea that I could try and understand how people chose to eat their food, and what that could tell me about them. I realised through the course that qualitative interviewing cannot pre-suppose anything about its participants and I continue to find that valuable in a wonderful way.
I settled on London as the urban site where cultures would likely coalesce. I settled on the South Asian diaspora as a broad, yet specific cultural space. And I settled on students living on their own as my subjects.
As I worked on designing the methodology for this, I realised that by allowing myself to conduct an “informal conversation interview” (Patton, 2002; Wells, 2011) when my participants were cooking their food in their kitchens, I could emplace myself in the interview environment and therefore allow the project to assume an auto-ethnographic tone through a “reflexive dyadic interview” (Ellis et al., 2011). This allowed me to bring myself back into the research, while also letting me keep my distance and not conflate the experiences of the subject with my own lived experience.
Some thoughts about method
The existence of research is validated through what value it adds to the world. And with qualitative research, I have come to realise that it is not a pursuit of objectivity, but the doubling down on subjectivity itself as being the thing that gives it its value. To quote Donna Haraway, ‘…my problem, and “our” problem, is how to have simultaneously an account of radical historical contingency for all knowledge claims and knowing subjects, a critical practice for recognising our own “semiotic technologies” for making meanings, and a no-nonsense commitment to faithful accounts of a “real” world…’ (Haraway, 1988). In my approach to this project, it was some such accounts of the real world that I was trying to discover - but also that while there is no such thing as objectivity, there still has to be unbiased commitment to acknowledging subjectivities to create something more objective, although not necessarily truly objective.
For instance, coding an interview cannot cherry-pick things people said that the researcher agrees with – it has to be objective in its holistic attempt at understanding everything a person said. But does the research itself have to strive for objectivity when objectivity itself is impossible? I didn’t think so. And therefore, through my project, I have doubled down on the biases created by my own relationship with food by emplacing myself in the research, rather than to remove myself from the field, while providing as free and open-ended an environment as possible for the participant to speak. My positionality and situation, therefore, has added nuance.
For this project, my engagement with narrative ethics enormously influenced my process. Arthur Frank (2014) talks of how ‘People not only live their lives, but also imagine the lives they are living, and stories express those imaginations. We humans act on what we have imagined even as actions shape our imaginations.’ I was interested in these narratives and stories, and had also interacted with the author before coming across the 2014 paper. In The Wounded Storyteller (Arthur Frank, 1995), the author conducts epistemological research into understanding medical patients and the wounds they carry, but the interesting complication is that the author himself had just recovered from an illness before he started working on the book. It seemed auto-ethnographic in the way my research was shaping itself to be.
The third chapter of Kathleen Wells’ book on Narrative Enquiry (2011) directed me to more of this kind of work. It provided me with a framework for my research that allowed me to imagine data outside of the interview. I engaged with the transcription of my interviews in a more interpretive way. I wrote down notes before, during and after each interview. I will discuss these more in detail in the next section.
I complicated these sources with my readings of John Law (2004) and Sarah Pink (2015). Law freed my mind. Where I was otherwise finding a model of a project someone else had conducted and trying to do something similar, I realised that my project was unique to me, and while I could rely on other ethnographies and research projects instructionally, I had the freedom to improvise. I allowed myself the space to transcribe elements in my recordings I thought were personally important, as can be seen in both the interview transcripts. That research was an inherently “messy” endeavour, and that it was involved in producing the very knowledge it was trying to discover was instrumental in shaping this project.
Reading Sarah Pink, specifically the section of her book (2015) where she talks about ‘Intentionally joining others in (near) universal embodied activities),’ like eating food was also relevant to my method design. By talking to my participants about how they ate food while they cooked it and while they were present with me in their kitchen, I was including myself in a sensory experience that was unique. The experiences of sight, sound and taste were all data, not simply the words that were being exchanged.
I therefore realised through engaging with these sources, that I would choose to conduct a semi-structured and open-ended interview. I could create space for both formal and informal interaction and wherever I wanted, could include parts of the research I felt relevant through my own embodiment in these environments. Interviews became the most ideal way to conduct my research. I could not do participant observation because it seemed logistically complicated to just put myself in someone’s kitchen without any intention. I do not think I would have been allowed there, neither would I have felt personally comfortable. I did consider asking participants to record themselves cooking and conduct the interview later in a more controlled environment, but I eventually decided I wanted to put myself in the field and see how it went. And so it all came together in that format.
The interview: before, during and after
Karen O’Reilly’s book on Ethnographic Methods (2005) presents the formality of interviews on a spectrum of very informal in terms of chance encounters in an ethnographic study, and a more intentional formal interview where the researcher is in control. I believe my research would fall in the latter category, where although the interview being conducted in a kitchen has potential for informal conversation, it is still, most decidedly, an interview where questions are being asked within a particular structure. As I noticed after conducting my three interviews, the third interview had a lot more informal interaction because my participant was getting regularly distracted by something in her environment, while this wasn’t the case in my second interview. In my first interview, my participant seemed to be aware of when the voice recorder was running, and was likely to spiral into informal conversation between recordings.
Before
I have changed the names of my interview participants to Sanah, Nidhi and Falguni. This is the order in which I conducted their interviews. I contacted Sanah and Falguni by asking them to meet me in person, while I contacted Nidhi (who I had a prior friendship with) on WhatsApp. I had no choice but to tell them all that I was vegan because all of them asked me what I wanted to eat. While Sanah chose to cook something non-vegetarian despite that (intending to preserve authenticity), Nidhi insisted that when she cooked with friends (which she perceived me as, despite my intention to be an objective interviewer) she would cook to accommodate them (which she thought was authentic of her), which I think is inherently interesting because it represented two different approaches to authenticity, and both were arguably ethically sound - which fits directly into my focus on narrative ethics.
I was afraid my participants knowing I was vegan would affect the research, but I believe that was the point of my research where I include myself in the process somewhat. When I explained the project to Falguni, she ended up having a long conversation with me about her food choices, which I later committed to field notes and was aware of when I conducted her interview. In that informal meeting, she mentioned her relationship with food growing up, all of which came unprompted, and it rattled me a little because I didn’t know what parts of this was data, because this wasn’t the interview itself, it was simply me telling her about my research question, but what I later recorded as field notes, from this informal conversation, also allowed me to plan a direction for the interviews when I finally got around to doing them.
During
I conducted the interviews relying on a pre-prepared structure of questions I allowed myself to deviate from wherever required, by taking jottings of my own experiences, and being aware of my own sensory involvement, namely of the space and the informal interactions we would have outside of the interview itself. For instance I was caught off guard in the first interview because I did not expect the table to be far away from the cooking area, and had to move around a lot because I needed a place to keep my things. I was also very surprised by the kinds of discomfort I felt when Sanah was cooking chicken. The overwhelming experience also disoriented me and made me pause and stammer a lot during the interview. While both Sanah and Falguni did not have discomfort talking while cooking (they seemed at ease and comfortable), Nidhi was unable to do both at the same time, so a large part of the interview happened sitting down, and the cooking after. Finally, I have to mention that it felt convenient to conduct multiple short recordings rather than one long recording (this was suggested to me by Sanah in the first interview, another classic example of the interviewee influencing method, and I stuck to this throughout).
After
I approached the transcription process as an intentional, interpretive act (Riessmann, 2008). I recognised and included pauses and expressive sounds in brackets, interruptions with hyphens and I used double quotes whenever interviewees paraphrased something someone else said. I recognised overlapping conversation wherever I could, as well as tonal changes (Wells, 2011; Poland, 2002).
In my analysis, I identified broad, unchanging inductive themes and flexible sub-codes within those themes while I was transcribing, and I assigned a colour to each theme. I gave myself some flexibility in terms of the sub-codes depending on each interview. For example, under the theme “social and cultural position”, Falguni exhibited a “proclivity to ethical consumption” practices that Sanah explicitly did not, so I used that sub-code specifically for the former. Similarly, I used the sub-code “cultural integration” for Sanah, that I could not for Falguni. But the theme is the same in both cases.
An interesting theme I used was Informal conversation, where I highlight conversations that occur outside of the interview that indicate the external environment. In this regard, I also would like to highlight that my conversation with Sanah in particular saw a lot of informal conversation in between recordings, where she would speak to me much more naturally when the recorder was off for a minute or so between recordings, and part of one such conversation got recorded in one of my recordings.
Very interestingly, in all three interviews, I found that the most exciting responses came at the end of the interview when I asked the participants if they wanted to add anything I had not asked. I found it disconcerting to ask questions about caste, or religion beyond a preliminary point, and was surprised to find Sanah bring it up herself. Similarly with Falguni, who spoke about wanting to be vegan herself.
Preliminary findings and observations
Since this is based on only three interviews, nothing I say in this part of the report is a generalised truth. Rather, I am mapping tensions, and highlighting dynamics that deserve more thought in policy. Preliminary analysis has offered insight into how food practices intersect with identity, politics and adaptation among South Asian students navigating life in London. One emerging theme is the role of social and familial relationships in shaping food-related decisions. Participants often described tensions with peers or family - particularly around political or religious beliefs, which influenced how they engage with food and others. In some cases, these tensions led to a form of quiet resistance, while in others, they contributed to a sense of disconnection or fatigue.
Another significant pattern is the varying degrees of adaptability participants showed in response to their new food environments. For example, while Falguni approaches food with strong political consciousness, also compounded by the field notes I recorded, she expresses deep discomfort with the compromises she’s had to make in London. In contrast, Sanah, who appears less politically invested in her food choices, reports a smoother adjustment and even a sense of liberation in her new routines.
Another interesting observation from Sanah’s interview is how it acts as an exception to the rule in terms of how regional, class and urban identities shape perceptions of dietary norms. Having grown up in an elite household in Kerala, although not exactly urban, she expresses genuine surprise at the prevalence of vegetarianism among her Indian peers outside of her hometown, and abroad. In the interview, she notes: “…That people don’t eat non-veg as much in India. So when we traveled around Europe with my family a little bit, we were taken for vegetarians a lot and I was actually very confused. I’m like ‘Huh? Okay, is it that common to be a vegetarian in India?’” This moment of bewilderment reveals the limitations of her prior understanding of Indian dietary practices, shaped by her own regional and caste-based experience.
Kerala occupies a unique position in India’s culinary and political landscape. While many Indian states, particularly in the north, have caste-linked taboos around meat, Kerala’s food culture is more diverse, with meat consumption cutting across caste and religion in many communities. As someone deeply embedded in this context, Sanah did not anticipate that vegetarianism - often seen in India as a marker of caste privilege and Brahminical purity - would be so dominant among her South Asian peers in London, or in Bangalore, where she worked for a few years prior to moving to London. While her assumption that most of India is vegetarian is false (Biswas, 2018), it seems true if one’s experience of the country is restricted to its urban spaces, which are largely made up of upper caste, privileged Indians. 
This misrecognition, while brief, is telling. It underscores how urban elite positionality in India (or in Sanah’s case, even non-urban spaces if you’re from Kerala) can foster a perception of culture that is contextually grounded but not universally applicable. Sanah’s politically engaged worldview, informed by her upbringing in Kerala, did not extend to a broader understanding of the cultural politics of food in other parts of India. In fact, her encounter with diasporic vegetarianism abroad reveals how Indian identity itself fragments when displaced - how assumptions carried from home are unsettled by the plurality of other Indian regions, or other international regions with Indian population.
These insights suggest that food practices among international students cannot be reduced to a single diasporic experience. Instead, they are shaped by local histories, caste structures and regional ideologies, which travel unevenly and often encounter friction in transnational contexts. If I were to return to this project in the future, I might explore these negotiations with more depth, particularly how ideological dissonance within diasporic spaces influence daily habits, consumption ethics and broader relationships to cultural identity.
References
Biswas, S., (2018), The myth of the Indian vegetarian nation, BBC. www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-india-43581122
Ellis, C., Adams, T. E., & Bochner, A. P. (2015). Autoethnography: an overview. Astrolabio (Cordoba), 14, 249–273.
Frank, A. W., ProQuest, & ProQuest. (1995). The wounded storyteller body, illness, and ethics. University of Chicago Press.
Frank, A. W. (2014). Narrative Ethics as Dialogical Story-Telling. The Hastings Center Report, 44(s1), S16–S20. doi.org/10.1002/hast.263
Haraway, D. (1988). Situated Knowledges: The Science Question in Feminism and the Privilege of Partial Perspective. Feminist Studies, 14(3), 575–599. doi.org/10.2307/3178066
Harvey, D. (2012). Rebel Cities: From the Right to the City to the Urban Revolution (1st ed., pp. xviii–xviii). Verso Books.
Holston, J., & Appadurai, A. (1996). Cities and Citizenship. Public Culture, 8(2), 187–204. doi.org/10.1215/08992363-8-2-187
Law, John (2004). Scientific Practices. In After Method: mess in social science research (pp. 18-44). Routledge Publications
Law, John (2011). Making a mess with method. In The Sage Handbook of Social Science Methodology. doi.org/10.4135/9781848607958
O’Reilly, Karen (2005). Interviews: asking questions of individuals or groups. In Ethnographic Methods (Second ed., pp. 116-140). Routledge Publications.
Patton, M. (2002). Qualitative research and evaluation methods (Third ed.). Sage Publications.
Pink, S. (2015). The sensoriality of the interview : rethinking personal encounters through the senses. In Doing Sensory Ethnography ( Second ed., pp. 73-93). SAGE Publications Ltd, doi.org/10.4135/9781473917057
Pink, S. (2015). Sensory research through participation : From observation to intervention. In Doing Sensory Ethnography (Second ed., pp. 107-116). SAGE Publications Ltd.
Poland, B. (2002). Transcription quality. In J. Gubrium & J. Holstein (Eds.), Handbook of interview research: Context and method (pp. 629-650). Thousand Oaks, Sage Publications.
Riessman, C. (2008). Narrative methods for the human sciences. Thousand Oaks. Sage Publications.
Rothgerber, Hank. (2020). Meat-related cognitive dissonance: A conceptual framework for understanding how meat eaters reduce negative arousal from eating animals. Appetite, Vol. 146, 104511, ISSN 1095-6663, doi.org/10.1016/j.appet.2019.104511. (www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195666319306324)
Wells, Kathleen. (2011). Framework for Collection of Narrative Data. In Narrative Enquiry (pp. 23-42). Pocket Guides to Social Work Research Methods. Oxford University Press.

Queering-Barthes.title: Queering Barthes - Science Fiction and the WWE
Queering-Barthes.content:
INTRODUCTION: Queering and Cripping Wrestling Mythologies
PART 1: The Myth of the Straight Fight – Queering Barthes through Punk vs. Cena
PART 2: Setting the Stage – CM Punk vs John Cena, 2011
PART 3: Unscripted Bodies – Queering and Cripping the Wrestling Ring
PART 4: An Affective Edge – Cyborgs and Monsters as Prefigurative Myth
Bibliography



home
other writing


This essay was originally submitted as part of graded coursework at the London School of Economics on 15 May 2025 for which I received a distinction grade. It is one of the weirdest, most fulfilling things I’ve ever written.
Introduction: Queering and Cripping Wrestling Mythologies
The World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE) is a global sports entertainment programme which features live, fictionalised performances of professional wrestling storylines adapted to audience interests to generate viewership (Barrett & Levin, 2013). The focus of this essay is on one such storyline – the CM Punk vs. John Cena storyline from the WWE’s 2011 “Money in the Bank” match. Through analysing this cultural object, this essay will attempt to queer the WWE as a sports entertainment storytelling medium. The WWE is a unique media form in that it has historically blurred the lines between what is scripted and what is real, and it is in the middle of this blurring that audience engagement is at its maximum. Real life feuds often make it into the scripts, and the audience themselves, stylised as the “WWE Universe” are often treated as part of the story by the various wrestlers who act as either heroes or villains (in WWE parlance, “babyfaces” or “heels” respectively) (Goggin & Emmanouloudis, 2019). The wrestling performances are less about the sport itself, and more about spectacle, engaging in grand theatrical showcases of masculinised stereotypes of strength and weakness, anger and sorrow, forgiveness and revenge.
In my essay, I begin by queering Roland Barthes’ concept of myth (1972), showing how the Cena vs. Punk storyline destabilises the very signs that structure wrestling’s cultural narratives. Through queer and crip theory, I read the match not as a straightforward contest of dominance, but as a space where masculinity, ability and meaning itself become unstable. In this slippage, I argue, lie possibilities for alternative victories, ones rooted in vulnerability, refusal and performative excess.
To explore these alternative victories, I then analyse the aesthetics of the fighting body in media that is queer-coded. I do this by exploring the popular speculative science fiction works of Sonnie’s Edge from Love, Death + Robots (2019) and the anime Cyberpunk: Edgerunners (2022). I suggest that the use of queer and posthuman tropes in science fiction offer new possibilities for how wrestling might represent bodies that are trans or gender non-conforming, cyborg, or otherwise in excess of conventional form.
Through my essay, I wonder (speculate) if there can ever be queer or crip bodies in the WWE, a historically hyper-masculine show, representing a historically hyper-masculine sport, and I wonder what those bodies might look like. I explore, most importantly, through Massumi (2002), how affect theory might act as a medium that enables this speculation. Alison Kafer’s work and Donna Haraway’s “Camille Stories” (2016) strongly inspire my essay in how I attempt to speculate a future grounded in queer and crip values that are not expressed in terms of heteronormative and able-bodied conceptions of time and space. Recalling Kafer, “In imagining more accessible futures, I am yearning for an elsewhere—and, perhaps, an ‘elsewhen’—in which disability is understood otherwise: as political, as valuable, as integral.”
Part 1: The Myth of the Straight Fight – Queering Barthes through Punk vs. Cena
In his 1957 essay, The World of Wrestling (in Mythologies, translated 1972), Roland Barthes famously argued that wrestling is not a sport but a moral spectacle: a theatre of exaggerated gestures where ambiguity collapses and meaning appears fixed. But unlike the wrestling Barthes wrote about, where performed aspects are portrayed as real, WWE does not hide its performativity, it embraces and thrives on it. What Barthes saw as hidden – the scripted, ideologically-loaded nature of the fight, is now flaunted. Terms like kayfabe (keeping the pretense that a fake performance is real), heel turns (portraying a wrestler as the villain), and babyface pushes (portraying a wrestler as the hero) have become part of the fan lexicon (Goggin & Emmanouloudis, 2019), circulated openly through online forums, fan discourse and social media.
But myth, for Barthes, is a distortion, not a lie. When something appears hidden, it is not. Myth transforms History into Nature, i.e. it takes something with a historical cultural origin (like gender roles, class dynamics, or in this case, hyper-masculine conventions of justice or heroism in wrestling) and presents it as if it were infallible truth. While it does not hide the “real” completely, it re-presents reality in a way that makes it seem an overwhelmingly indisputable reflection of reality.
But because this is a distortion (not a seamless illusion), the edges of the myth can be seen, and it reveals its own mechanisms, even as it tries to obscure them. This is the space where I argue that queer and crip theory can intervene. By not rejecting Barthes, but reorienting his mythic logic, I argue that contemporary wrestling and sports entertainment at large represent meaning in a way that is unstable, contested and open to disruption. The Punk vs. Cena storyline is an ideal example of this, where the myth is undone and remade in an unconventional way.
Wrestling today exists in a hypermediated space: where the line between reality and fiction is not just blurred, but constantly re-negotiated in public. The result is a spectacle that is deeply aware of its own constructedness – a myth that knows it is a myth, but still functions as if it were true. Very often in WWE, attempted storylines fail, or are altered after they are conceived because the fans simply refused to be entertained by them.
Articles about these storylines are routinely published and read, and comment sections of YouTube videos uploaded by the WWE are rife with opinions of fans that approve or disapprove of turns a story has taken, all the time being aware that it is a story, but one they are able to embody and influence in ways that are different from other storytelling formats.
In this context, Barthes remains a useful, but insufficient theorist for understanding wrestling today. His insight that wrestling transforms History into Nature still holds, but the mechanisms through which the transformation occurs have shifted. The WWE, in the digital age, has embraced the artificiality of its narratives, creating layered performances in which authenticity is both staged and questioned. The myth is no longer stable or singular. It flickers, glitches, multiplies. And it is within this instability that the 2011 Punk vs. Cena storyline emerges as a critical site for analysis. Rather than rendering Barthes obsolete, this moment allows us to re-read him with queer/crip lenses – not as a theorist of stability, but as one who inadvertently reveals the cracks.
Part 2: Setting the Stage – CM Punk vs John Cena, 2011
On July 17, 2011, WWE’s Money in the Bank pay-per-view hosted one of the most iconic main events in WWE history: CM Punk vs. John Cena for the WWE Championship. At first glance, this might appear like any other scripted match within WWE’s sprawling entertainment machine. But the storyline leading up to this moment, and the match itself, signalled something more significant (WWE, 2011).
Kayfabe – the illusion of realism in wrestling is critical here. As a performative frame, kayfabe establishes the terms of engagement for both performer and audience (Goggin & Emmanouloudis, 2019). It signals that while what you are watching is fake, the stakes are real. Emotions, injuries, allegiances, all blend within this liminal zone of suspended disbelief. Punk vs. Cena complicates this concept in its narrative, where kayfabe and reality appear to collapse into one another – a Barthesian spectacle rewritten through aesthetics of rebellion, authenticity, and anti-corporate sentiment.
CM Punk (real name Phillip Brooks), cast as the rebel outsider, delivers what fans now call “The Pipebomb” – a promo (refers to a scripted or improvised speech delivered by a wrestler to build narrative tension, develop character or provoke an opponent) that breaks character, criticising WWE’s management, its formulaic storytelling and the commodification of wrestler’s identities (WWE, 2025). The promo blurs the lines between real and scripted, destabilising the viewer’s understanding of what is performance and what is truth. Towards the end of this rant, the network turns his microphone off. He stands up, screams into the camera and storms out of the building.
While Punk did have an approved script, he chose not to adhere to it and used it as an opportunity to say what he truly felt. “The thing with live television is, it’s easier to ask for forgiveness than permission,” he said in an interview he gave after the fact (Taylor, 2021). In Barthes’ terms, Punk’s actions rupture the seamless myth, calling attention to the fact that what is usually masked (“the intelligibility of reality”) is now spoken aloud. He names the mechanics of the system from within the ring itself.
Barthes argues that wrestling converts ideological positions into spectacle, transforming what is a possible eventuality into inevitable reality. Punk’s character – and perhaps his real-life position – attempts to resist that. Yet even within this resistance, he becomes mythologised. WWE reabsorbs his anti-establishment posture into its own promotional material. The audience is encouraged to cheer for his rebellion while still buying tickets to see it. Punk, once the underdog critic, becomes the central figure of the WWE’s narrative universe – legitimised through the very institution he critiqued.
On the other side of the ring stands John Cena: the company man, the straight-talking face of the WWE brand, cisgendered-heteronormative, white, echoing slogans like “Hustle, Royalty, Respect.” In the moral binary of traditional wrestling storytelling, Cena is the babyface, the hero, and Punk is the unpredictable outsider. But Money in the Bank complicated these roles. The crowd in Chicago that night overwhelmingly supported Punk, booing Cena not for villainy, but for alignment with the status quo. Cena’s body, normally coded as virtuous, clean, and muscular: the archetypal American man – became a symbol of institutional conservatism. Punk’s thinner, tattooed, chaotic body was no longer the heel; it was the people’s hero. In the future, Punk would be successfully marketed as a “Voice for the Voiceless.” This reversal reveals how the morality of spectacle is not fixed, but negotiated – a negotiation that audiences participate in.
Thus, what appears to be a straightforward match, a championship fight between two stars, becomes a field of dense semiotic play. The match ends with Punk winning the title and escaping through the crowd, never to return, after blowing a kiss at Vince McMahon, the manager of WWE (who also performs a character: a villain, the horrible capitalist boss “Mr. McMahon”). From the start of this storyline, fans have been made aware that Punk’s contract was expiring that year – but are unsure whether this is real or part of the story. As a Barthesian myth, the moment collapses the naturalisation of order. Punk doesn’t just win the fight, he symbolically ruptures the system. But as Barthes would remind us, this rupture is always temporary. Myth absorbs all rebellion. Punk returned to the company weeks later, his victory packaged into merchandise and marketing.
Yet the fact that audiences responded so viscerally – that Punk’s pipebomb felt “real” to so many – reveals the hunger for myth to shift. Punk vs. Cena represented not just a moral struggle, but a meta-narrative about wrestling’s own institutions. It asked whether myth can be hacked from within, and whether the spectacle can critique itself. Barthes may not have used the language of queerness, but his system hinges on readability and moral performance – both of which are sites of destabilisation in queer/crip theory. This moment queers not just wrestling, but Barthes’ own semiotic logic.
Part 3: Unscripted Bodies – Queering and Cripping the Wrestling Ring
“Autism is an ability and a disability: it is as much about what is abundant as what is missing.” This quote by Phil Collins in Stuart Murray’s 2013 essay Autism and the Posthuman, hints at how autism outhumans the humans, and “gestures towards a beyond, or a ‘post-,’ that represents a space of cultural enactment, and possibly agency, separate from any configuration of a human norm (Murray, 2013).”
While my essay is not about autism particularly, the second quote in the earlier paragraph, is relevant. Focussing on disability and queerness in general, Kafer (2013) and Halberstam (2005) have explored how it is fundamentally necessary that conceptions of right and wrong, success and failure, be conceptualised in contexts that are not able-bodied or queer, and in Murray’s essay, disabled bodies are framed as posthuman, specifically doing that. Thinking of queer/crip bodies in fighting contexts is not about putting queer fighters who are “strong” in the ring, it is about reframing what strength itself is.
Barthes’ myth relies on a semiological structure. Drawing from Saussure’s linguistics, Barthes conceptualises the “signifier” as the material form (word, image, gesture) and the “signified” as the concept it evokes. Together, they form the “sign” (Barthes, 1972, pp 106-126). In “myth,” however, this unity is reappropriated. Barthes’ structure distorts a pre-existing sign into something ideologically loaded. The original sign becomes a signifier in a new system, where the thing that is signified (some cultural form or idea) is presented as nature. Myth, makes the contingent seem inevitable. Which is why Barthes uses wrestling as his example: it is a form where moral narratives are communicated through exaggerated physical codes that appear self-evident, reducing historical complexity into a spectacle of clarity.
But it is in spectacle’s self-evidence that cracks begin to show. If myth aims to stabilise meaning, to collapse the distance between signifier and signified, then queer and crip theory thrive in the failure of this very project. Judith Butler (1990) writes about how gender is never a completed identity, but a reiterated performance, whose repetition is vulnerable to breakdown. Eve Sedgwick (1990) discusses how sexuality resists binary logics, existing in gaps between identity and expression. Robert McRuer (2006) argues that “compulsory able-bodiedness” is, very similarly to heterosexuality, propped up by normativity but always haunted by its own instability. There is always a potential for slippage (Sedgwick, 1990, p. 29) between meaning and body, word and flesh, identity and performance.
This slippage is what allows a queering (and cripping) of the wrestling ring. It is not just the rewriting or unsettling of the excessiveness of wrestling as a bodily performance. What happens when the ring itself becomes a site of semantic rebellion? What happens when the performers no longer play into clearly defined roles, but instead expose the fragility of the narrative system itself?
In this context, CM Punk’s feud with John Cena in 2011, and the “Pipebomb” promo can be read as a queering of the Barthesian myth. Punk’s disruption of kayfabe reveals the artifice of the spectacle, while simultaneously amplifying its emotional truth. He is both “in character” and not. Both heel and face. Both employee and critic. His body leaner, tattooed, “non-standard,” resists the coded legibility of the heroic figure in traditional wrestling stories. Cena, on the other hand, is an archetype – the average jock – muscular, straight, disciplined. A body representing moral virtue and institutional conservatism.
Queer theory’s insistence on non-normativity helps us read Punk’s character, and body, as a challenge to dominant ideologies. In a Barthesian sense, the match is still mythic – it is a narrative of resistance being naturalised. But it fails, or at the very least, is complicated, because the meanings on offer are not unambiguous. The myth glitches. The audience cheers for the anti-hero. Punk’s rejection of the system is absorbed into the system, yes, but not without leaving something affective behind. This disturbance lingers. I will come back to this later in the essay.
Crip theory depends this analysis. McRuer (2006) critiques how compulsory able-bodiedness is upheld by capitalist culture, a logic that Kafer (2013) expands into the temporal realm through the articulation of crip (and queer) time, specifically with respect to how expectations of speed and productivity are disrupted by non-normative bodies engaging with space. This resonates with how Punk resists the scripted rhythms of WWE storytelling. He refuses to follow the beat. Even in the physical register, his body doesn’t “perform” wrestling the standard way. His very aesthetic thrives on unpredictability. Crip theory exposes how spectacle depends on smoothness, predictability and able-bodied assumption. Punk’s refusal to play by those rules marks a bodily resistance to the normative narrative.
Furthermore, the crowd that cheers Punk and boos Cena doesn’t merely respond to the script. Audiences refuse the moral binary, resisting the narrative. Punk’s myth is unstable because it is co-produced by those who do not align with its original message. What we witness is a form of communal queering – an audience excited by friction, contradiction, irony and improvisation.
While this reading may stretch the boundaries of what is considered queer, I am less interested in labelling the event itself as queer and more invested in how audiences in the WWE find themselves increasingly drawn to non-normative, unstable narratives. As dominant storylines (myths) are reproduced to the point of exhaustion, their original meanings begin to distort. This slippage opens space for reinterpretation where communal reception becomes an act of queering – whether intentional or not.
The failure of myth to fix meaning is not a shortcoming of Barthes’ theory – it is its most queer potential. His work anticipates this: myth is always at risk of being read against itself, turned inside out. This is even more clear in his later work, especially in The Death of the Author (1997), where he writes that meaning doesn’t come from the author’s intention but from the reader’s interpretation. Barthes may not have written in the language of queerness, but the instability he identifies, the gap between signifier and signified is fertile ground for queer and crip reading. Myth can never fully naturalise because it relies on performance. And all performance, as Butler teaches us, contains the possibility of failure, of deviation, of drag (Butler, 1990).
This essay does not reject Barthes, it queers him. Wrestling is not simply a stage on which myth is performed – it is a battleground where myth falters. What is at stake here is not whether wrestling can still be read through Barthes, but how Barthes’ theory can be extended through queer and crip thought. Punk’s body, his unscripted speech, his mythologised rebellion all offer an invitation to revisit the semiotics of the spectacle. We are not beyond Barthes. We are taking his concepts and dragging them through the ring, letting them sweat and slip and shatter. In doing so, we don’t abandon myth, we wrestle with it.
The 2011 match between Punk and Cena is not a tidy allegory of rebellion. It is a layered field of political, aesthetic and bodily tension. It thrives in what Sedgwick (1990) might call an epistemological uncertainty of performance. Queer and crip readings, here, are not only possible, but necessary.
Part 4: An Affective Edge – Cyborgs and Monsters as Prefigurative Myth
What stands out most effectively in the Punk vs. Cena story is that while it unsettles semiological structure, it retains something innate to the story – the way audiences are made to feel. This section will analyse the storyline from the episode, Sonnie’s Edge (Wilson & Pennacchioli, 2019) from Love, Death + Robots (2019) and the characters from the show Cyberpunk: Edgerunners (Jaki & Pondsmith, 2022). While Sonnie’s Edge originated as a short story in Peter F. Hamilton’s A Second Chance at Eden (1998), this essay focuses on the animated adaptation which radically intensifies the affective experience of the narrative.
In Sonnie’s Edge (2019), audiences watch as genetically engineered monster-like bodies engage in fighting matches, bodies controlled by their human owners who stand outside the ring. Sonnie pilots one such beast. She has never lost, and she is also the only female fighter. The twist in the story is that Sonnie’s consciousness is inside the creature (unlike the other fighters) and her human body is really a robot. She is not controlling the beast, she embodies it. If the beast dies, she dies too. And so, she fights through this monstrous other-body, but unlike the other fighters who are safe outside the ring, controlling their monsters remotely, Sonnie fights for her life. But to complicate this even further, she fights not merely to survive but also to exact revenge. We learn she was a survivor of sexual violence, and her physical body doesn’t exist anymore. She chooses to channel her violence through the body of a literal monster, whose grotesque strength enacts revenge on a system of violence she never opted into. As a narrative, Sonnie’s Edge is a classic tale of revenge, but the difference is, it is a woman who fights for herself, and with each subsequent fight, exacts revenge on the men who violated her.
Affect, as Massumi (2002) describes, is not what something means, but what it does. Unlike emotions like sadness or anger which are already coded and socially legible, affect exists as a bodily intensity, despite language. A revenge story is recognisable not just in its plot beats, but in the cathartic bodily response it produces. In Sonnie’s Edge, this affective response is preserved even as the narrative is queered, cripped and estranged. Sonnie’s real body was mutilated beyond recovery. She does not want to be “fixed,” because she physically cannot be, but by embodying this monstrous other-body, she is powerful, and she does not lose. Her revenge is not mediated through a normative masculine hero figure who will save her, yet the viewer feels the pulse of triumph when the abuser is harmed. The narrative signifiers have shifted, but the affective payload remains intact. This suggests, very importantly to this essay, that affect – unlike narrative – can survive radical reconfiguration. It is less a structure and more a charge, a shock that leaps across signification. Barthes hints at the affective potential of signs when he talks of the “message without a code” specifically when he writes about photography (Åker et. al., 2024).
Queer/crip theory has long troubled the logic of “overcoming.” Kafer (2013) critiques the temporal frameworks that define disabled people as recovering from something that needs to be cured – to live in a way that they are surviving despite their disability, measured over periods of time (longevity). McRuer (2006), similarly identifies compulsory able-bodiedness as a cultural myth – one that crumbles under its own contradictions. Sonnie’s refusal of restoration echoes these insights. Her body is not a site of pity, but of strategy. Her “edge” is not the condition of her victimhood, but the terrain from which she reclaims agency. In this way, Sonnie’s Edge queers the fight itself. It displaces the fantasy of able-bodied dominance with an erotic, feral, posthuman rage. Her monstrosity is not a deviation from the human, but subverts what the category of human has historically excluded.
Similarly, Cyberpunk: Edgerunners (2022) is an anime set in the universe of the popular video game Cyberpunk 2077 (2020), based on the work of board and video game designer Michael Pondsmith. The anime presents a world in which bodies are endlessly modifiable, and identity is shaped by one’s capacity to interface with cybernetic enhancement. David, the protagonist, increasingly sacrifices pieces of his organic body in the pursuit of power, protection and purpose. His transformation is gradual, but devastating. By the series’ end, he is no longer recognisably human. His enhancements affect his brain, causing him to go “over the edge.” He is a tragic figure, not because he “loses” his humanity, but because he is consumed by a system in which enhancing his body is the only way he will be able to live the life he wants to – his class position making it impossible for him to climb the socioeconomic ladder any other way. His downfall, therefore, is not cybernetics, but the capitalist logic that demands their constant escalation.
Within this setting, Edgerunners also shows us, through its large set of characters – alternative embodiment. Characters like Rebecca – short, brash, heavily modded and carrying an enormous array of machine guns; or Dorio, the female character with a non-normative gender presentation reminiscent of butch/queer iconography – these characters are not failed versions of a masculine ideal, they are their own signs. They interrupt the fantasy of the sleek, perfect cyber-body and replace it with a chaotic aesthetic that is unpredictable, uncontainable and queer. These characters hint at Halberstam’s “queer art of failure” (2011). These are bodies that succeed not by conforming but by refusing the terms of success altogether.
In response to theories that frame information as disembodied or purely patterned, Murray (2013, p. 68) insists on the importance of “presence” – referring to the material, embodied reality of “being.” That there is value and power to being, even if that being doesn’t make sense to normative systems. Reading Murray and Massumi together, they foreground my argument of how non-normative bodies, like those in Sonnie’s Edge or Edgerunners, matter not because of what they signify, but because of what they do, what they feel like, and how they rupture scripted meaning.
In both these media, the fighting body is no longer legible through binaries of strong/weak, hero/villain. It is a site of excess. Myth has always relied on fragile foundations, sustained through repetition, but as Butler reminds us, repetition is inherently unstable.
The crux of the argument is this: the myth of the strong body, like the myth of the “straight fight” begins to unmake itself through its own excesses. The more it is performed, the more its signs (babyface, heel, hero, monster, machine) collapse into one another. What once signified dominance now signals instability. As WWE demonstrates, when fans reject the moral narrative they’re offered, when they cheer for the heel and boo the babyface, the signs start glitching. The myth queers itself, while the affect remains the same or is made more visceral.
Media like Sonnie’s Edge and Edgerunners show us that audiences are already engaging with non-normative bodies in fighting narratives. They already enjoy them. Even when these bodies are framed within traditional arcs of sacrifice and tragedy, they crack open new possibilities for meaning. They make space for other kinds of power, other “edges.” They are not just texts to be analysed, but futures being rehearsed.
While it is tempting to read these texts as entirely subversive, I argue that as they still remain affectively legible, they are just as effective as what they subvert. These works don’t queer and crip intentionally, they do so by rewriting the signs, rather than reinventing them, which is why audiences of all kinds are able to enjoy them. The viewer still feels the revenge in Sonnie’s Edge, still mourns David’s fall in Edgerunners. These affective arcs are recognisable, even when narratives have been radically rearranged. This is crucial. As Massumi says (2002), “belief has waned, but not affect.” We may no longer fully trust the classic revenge arc or the tragedy of the noble hero, but we still respond to the shape of those intensities. These stories trade in familiar feelings, even though they scramble their causes. They offer a kind of affective déjà vu. This opens the possibility for myth not to be destroyed, but to be recharged – queered through affective continuity.
Part 5: Conclusion – Wrestling Entertainment in a Posthuman Future
What might wrestling look like in a world that no longer pretends to be neutral? What if the bodies in the ring, once sculpted around fantasies of masculinity, whiteness and able-bodied dominance were glitchy, uneven and unruly? Would the audience still cheer? Would the story succeed?
Haraway (2016), specifically in Camille Stories, what she calls a “speculative fabulation,” reminds us that “staying with the trouble” requires imagining otherwise – speculative worldings that are not utopian but grounded in complexity. Kafer (2013) similarly calls for accessible futures that centre disabled lives not as problems to fix, but as frameworks to reimagine the world. The core idea for this essay originated in my wondering if the WWE could have trans bodies, or disabled bodies, and the more I read about the WWE, many stories like Punk vs. Cena caught my eye as being complex, postmodern reinterpretations of what wrestling used to be. What if I were to take the bodies from Cyberpunk: Edgerunners, or a storyline like Sonnie’s Edge and run it in the WWE Universe?
What if CM Punk was an AFAB trans fighter (I am going to call her Punk.exe) with a body threaded through with neuroplastic wiring, an aesthetic of asymmetry, mess, glitch? What if her being a “survivor” of sexual assault was a part of her real-life backstory? What if her story was in a world where the original Punk vs. Cena had already happened?
What if John Cena (or some other name, like Jack Vega) was a white, muscular, state-sponsored hero figure, but what if he had prosthetic legs? Or, since I’m taking creative liberties anyway, some sort of cybernetic rollerblades for legs, rolling through the ring with kinetic elegance? Would he still be the “face of the company,” or would his posthuman body be too unreadable for traditional kayfabe?
What if this story was a recursion of the Punk vs Cena storyline, re-coded through bodies that had already failed to conform? What if the WWE had implemented layers of algorithmic oversight to prevent another unscripted promo, but Punk.exe found a way to speak to audiences anyway?
None of these scenarios are narratively coherent, and perhaps they shouldn’t be. But they allow us to ask: how much of narrative’s power comes from the story, and how much from the affective charge of the bodies that inhabit it?
Speculation, then, is not an escape – it is a commitment. A way to hold open the contradictions spectacle tries to seal. This essay has argued that wrestling, specifically in the Punk vs. Cena storyline – offers a mythic stage where normativity is both reinforced and disrupted. Through queer and crip theory, I have read these ruptures not as accidents, but as slippages – produced by the overuse of mythic signs. Through speculative fiction, I ask what new myths might form when queer and disabled bodies take centre stage. What would a myth look like if it didn’t centre perfection, but excess; if it didn’t embolden clarity, but prioritised affect instead; if it didn’t incentivise restoration, but made a spectacle of the refusal to need restoring? Perhaps a future cannot exist until it is imagined. 
Bibliography
Åker, P., Ericson, S., Bengtsson, S., & Stiernstedt, F. (2024). Roland Barthes (1980) Camera Lucida. In Classics in Media Theory (1st ed., pp. 268–283). Routledge. doi.org/10.4324/9781003432272-20
Barrett, B. J., & Levin, D. S. (2014). What’s Love Got to Do with It? A Qualitative Grounded Theory Content Analysis of Romance Narratives in the PG Era of World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE) Programming. Sexuality & Culture, 18(3), 560–591. doi.org/10.1007/s12119-013-9211-4
Butler, J. (1990). Gender trouble : feminism and the subversion of identity. Routledge.
Barthes, R., & Lavers, A. (1972). World of Wrestling. In Mythologies. The Noonday Press.
Goggin, J., & Emmanouloudis, A. (2019). The Pro Wrestling Audience as Imagined Community: Reflecting on the WWE Universe as a “Fan-Generated Narrative” Body. In C. D. Reinhard, & C. J. Olson (Eds.), Convergent Wrestling: Participatory Culture, Transmedia Storytelling, and Intertextuality in the Squared Circle (pp. 136-148). (The Cultural Politics of Media and Popular Culture). Routledge. doi.org/10.4324/9781351233989-11
Halberstam, J. (2005). Queer Temporality and Postmodern Geographies. In a Queer Time and Place: Transgender Bodies, Subcultural Lives (1st ed., Vol. 3, pp. viii–viii). NYU Press. doi.org/10.18574/nyu/9780814790892
Halberstam, J. (2011). The queer art of failure. Duke University Press.
Hamilton, P. F. (1998). Sonnie’s Edge. In A Second Chance at Eden. Macmillian.
Haraway, D. J. (2016). The Camille Stories – Children of Compost. In Staying with the trouble : making kin in the Chthulucene. Duke University Press
Kafer, A. (2013). Feminist, Queer, Crip (1st ed.). Indiana University Press.
Massumi, B. (2002). Parables for the virtual : movement, affect, sensation. Duke University Press.
McRuer, R. (2022). Introduction: Compulsory Able-Bodiedness and Queer/Disabled Existence. In Crip Theory (Vol. 9, pp. 1–32). New York University Press. doi.org/10.18574/nyu/9780814759868.003.0005
Stuart Murray, Orsini, M., & Davidson, J. (2013). Autism and the Posthuman. In Worlds of Autism (pp. 53-72). University of Minnesota Press. doi.org/10.5749/minnesota/9780816688883.003.0003
Newton, K.M. (1997). Roland Barthes: ‘The Death of the Author’. In: Newton, K.M. (eds) Twentieth-Century Literary Theory. Palgrave, London. doi.org/10.1007/978-1-349-25934-2_25
Jaki, R; Pondsmith. M. (2022). Cyberpunk: Edgerunners [TV Series]. CD Projekt; Netflix.
Wilson, D; Pennacchioli, G. (2019). Sonnie’s Edge in Miller, T. (2019). Love, Death & Robots. [TV Episode]. Blur Studio; Netflix.
Sanchez Taylor. (2021). CM Punk on how much of WWE Pipe Bomb Promo was Scripted. Wrestletalk. wrestletalk.com/news/cm-punk-pipe-bomb-scripted/#google_vignette
Sedgwick, E. K., & American Council of Learned Societies. (1990). Epistemology of the Closet, Updated with a New Preface : Updated with a New Preface(Second edition.). University of California Press.

about.title: More about me
about.content:
I have recently completed an MSc in Sociology (the course is called Culture & Society) at the London School of Economics and Political Science. I did my undergrad in law, and have some work experience in environmental and tech policy spaces. I am a qualitative researcher with a special interest in mix-method approaches to social sciences research. My work explores speculative methods, human-object relationships and ethics in technology and consumption. I am particularly interested in projects that challenge dominant narratives, democratise knowledge-production and use creative methodologies to think about global futures. I don’t look at methods as simply tools for data collection, I argue that they are narrative forms that can decentralise expertise, reimagine systems and surface ethical futures.
My personal animal liberation activism also influences my work, specifically in my perception of relationships the human species has with nonhuman entities, which has taught me to de-hierarchise some kinds of life over others, and refuse to privilege some issues as worthy of more consideration because of this. This posthuman political ecology bleeds into all of the research I participate in and is a fundamental defining characteristic. By bringing interdisciplinary research into this field (Pierre Bourdieu’s field), and using ethnographic and experimental methods, I explore questions like AI ethics, environmental futures, collective memory and techno-politics. I think that by employing radical approaches to analysing a field or a space, we can potentially see connections that spill over from such spaces (spaces you’d least expect them to spill over from), making everything seem like a complex monoculture with power being scattered all over the place. While a world like this can seem incomprehensible and hint at a depressing future, I think if we look closely at these systems and the narratives that drive them, there is potential to draft policy that can change things for the better.
I have experience conducting focus groups, interviews, analysing qualitative data and developing interdisciplinary frameworks that connect material culture, media and social transformation. My expertise lies in translating complex theoretical ideas into accessible, politically relevant research. With a strong interdisciplinary foundation, and a commitment to intellectual rigour, I am eager to contribute to innovative, socially impactful work.
Interests

Research Methodology (Interpretive Sociology)
Political Ecology (Multi-species Ethnography)
Narrative Ethics
Speculative and Playful Research Methods
Cultural Studies
Science and Technology Studies
Gender and Disability Studies

index.title: Home
index.content:
Speculative Sociologist | Qualitative Researcher | Narrative Ethicist
Hey, welcome to my tiny corner of the internet. Here’s a little bit about me. I’m a London based writer and sociologist, who is obsessed with narratives that shape culture. I’m even more obsessed with the potential for research to shape, alter and imagine better futures for everything in our material reality, both the human and the nonhuman. As a result of these obsessions, I spend my time thinking up and designing playful and complex ways to collect qualitative data from people and the world.

Here’s my LinkedIn
email: hello@samarth.ink

writing

my-work.title: My work (some of it)
my-work.content:
Animal Think Tank UK - Qualitative Research for Psychographic Segmentation (January 2026-present)
I am leading the qualitative research for a project involving psychographic segmentation of UK populations exploring ethical and moral relationships to animals. The project builds on quantitative research (which I assisted on, albeit in a small way) and tries to use focus groups infused with experimental methodology and narrative tools to measure opportunities for engagement with each segment. It’s pretty wild considering it has therefore become possible to understand surprisingly accurately the extent of involvement we can expect from a certain type of person - across race, class, gender and other demographic markers because what we’re measuring is the psychological profile of the person. This has been a superb project to be part of, especially within the animal freedom movement.
Using Storytelling as a Qualitative Research Method to explore Sociotechnical Futures (April-August 2025)
As part of my dissertation at the LSE, I investigated how cultural imaginaries shaped AI ethics in particular and technology in general using speculative storytelling as a method. For this project, I interviewed both experts and non-experts engaging them in a participatory methodology that encouraged them to imagine a future centering AI and technology in its current pace. I conducted about 11 interviews including one focus group. I built a custom HTML/JSS/JS front-end interface to gamify participant interviews and used a JSON-based API backend to log user choices in real time.
Write to me if you’d like to read a sample (or the entire thing if you’re so inclined).
Project on food, materiality and identity (October 2024-January 2025)
I designed and conducted a qualitative research project exploring South Asian relationships with food, memory and material culture. The project involved semi-structured interviews and participant observation incorporating sensory ethnographic methods. As part of this, I developed an auto-ethnographic component, critically reflecting on my own subjectivity and positionality in the context of the project. I then presented key findings in a research report for the LSE.
If you’d like to read about my process and what i discovered, here’s the report.
Essay on queering and cripping barthesian mythologies through wrestling entertainment
In this essay, I use the iconic 2011 WWE storyline between CM Punk and John Cena to subvert traditional Barthesian mythologies through the work of Butler, McRuer, Halberstam and Kafer. I then use the speculative fictions of Sonnie’s Edge and Cyberpunk: Edgerunners to imagine what fighting bodies might look like in a queercrip future: trans, disabled, monstrous, affective. I also explore the potential for such a shift, mainly through affect theory (Massumi).
If you’d like to read the essay, here’s the link.
CGEI - Project on climate change and women farmers (September 2023 to February 2024)
I interned a few months at the Centre for Gender and Environment India (CGEI). I was involved in editing and contributing to some of the research work they were engaged in. I notably published an article criticising an amendment to forest conservation law in India. The bulk of my work there was in collecting secondary data on government schemes concerning agriculture and climate change, women farmers, or both, as part of a project with the support of the University of Leeds concerning Climate Change and Women Farmers in Uttar Pradesh. My work complemented field work the firm was engaged in.
Gender Studies Committee, Christ University (2021-2023)
As convenor of my university’s Gender Studies Committee in 2023, as well as having been a core committee member two years prior, I gained organisational experience working with government bodies, drafting proposals, conducting events, seminars, etc. I was also the founding editor of the committee’s official newsletter, FLUID, and was involved in the publication of two subsequent issues.
BananaIP Counsels (June-July 2022)
i was hired as a legal intern. in terms of legal work in the intellectual property framework, i was involved in conducting patent background research on products, drafting trademark documentation, background searches on prior art and prior use, and a bunch of other stuff. most notably, i worked on a response to a Proposed Draft Amendment to IT Rules which the Indian government was seeking suggestions for at the time. here’s a link to what we came up with. i also independently sent in my own suggested amendments.
